item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really loved this one. It felt sad, and gentle. I really liked the meta-posting, too.

Easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This left me pretty unsettled, but my eyes kind of glossed over the logs. Novote for now until I can get deeper into the logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this what the Foundation thinks of (spiritual) fusion?!
(or maybe just what 3888 thinks… given that at least some of them appear to be forced…)

Or is it simply a very slow and limited version of the rapture?

it's totally The Cluster, is't it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather meandering and unambitious, but the atmosphere and the intrigue save it

Reluctant +1, eager for planned changes.

item.title: SCP-3888
item.content:encoded:
Moved to a new slot, made a few minor edits (for now), and I think I swapped out all of the 3000s for 3888s.

I'll probably be making some other edits around this… soon. Kinda busy for the next couple of days.

In terms of those who does feel it needs just a bit more explanation… I don't think the Foundation would have much else in their Level 1 clearance document, but I'm considering a companion tale to tie into this. Wouldn't explain everything, of course. No certain promises yet; maybe that would be A Mistake.

Thanks everybody who commented for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Besides being the name of a Steven Universe episode that made me cry, this has really good tone, and I like the concept a lot, including that you didn't give a Big Reveal (I love Big Reveals but this works a lot better without it).

The biggest issue for me is that the messages to the reader were too simplistic to compel me. I kept feeling really compelled by the logs themselves, then being knocked out of that mood by another 'eh' message from the entity.

The messages also mess up the feeling from the logs a bit; if these generic messages were really what caused them to want to leave, then I have to assume it was just doofy mind-control from space not-Jesus and nothing more than that, which isn't compelling at at.

I think the messages should be removed and replaced with something else.

You may need other elements to flesh this piece out more, too. Not sure what those would be, but something feels missing (and I don't mean that you don't explain what's really going on — the fact that you don't is currently one of the piece's strengths. It's not information that's missing, I don't think).

I'll go ahead and upvote just for the overall tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea of the anomoly affecting the reader and how it ends with the implication that you've been taken.

Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Platform Nine-and-Three Quarters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad that you liked it, and that you picked up on some of the overarching-themes-what-I-was-experimenting-with-hiding-in-plain-sight!

When/if (hopefully not if…) I get around to making some edits here, I'll definitely consider that criticism too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked it, instant upvote for me. It gives a good sense of the creeping onset of loneliness, and I liked the slow reveal that these people allowed themselves to disappear. That's the part that really spooked me.

My one criticism is that I would like to see one of the instances with 'signs of a struggle' mentioned in the Description. It makes sense as a Foundation document (an example of a rare but repeated event is useful to documentation), and it could be a good vehicle for displaying the person who makes a last desperate struggle against loneliness only to succumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the advice! Enough criticism comes from the vein of "I wish I could understand what was going on/I wish there was more of a payoff" that I was thinking of implementing option 2 in the near future. Not sure when I'll be done with… something… to edit in for that, but trying to preserve what some like about it while making it less of a chore for the reader to figure out the story is a tricky balance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this suffers from "vagueness limbo" aka it's both too vague and too explicit at the same time, which gives you two options.

1. Make it more vague. Get rid of the in-between text and have it just be about many people disappearing and no one knows why. This in and of itself is freaky and you have some cool stuff like the video logs. Maybe add in stuff about how panicked the Foundation is about what's going on because of the fact that they have no idea what's going on.

2. Make it less vague. Add more info on why people are being taken away. Maybe hint at a purpose they will serve. Something horrifying but at the same time necessary (thinking of Horizon: Zero Dawn and all that mess).

The core idea is definitely enough to make something really good. The content just needs tweaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan of mysteries where the clues only seem to be connected by the flimsiest threads, or format screws that are only given the flimsiest justification. 90% of this SCP basically just seemed like it was gesticulation meant to convince something spooky was going on without actually tying anything into a compelling or interesting story beyond the premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm. I like the idea, I really do… I just don't know if it feels resolved enough yet. I get that may be the point you're going for but it left me feeling confused, like I missed something.

I like the messages, I like the implication that whatever's doing this is going to do it to you, and I like the apathy-virus idea. It just drags at the end and doesn't give me any sort of payoff.

No vote, but I admire the effort. With a bit of tinkering this could be stellar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it's not scary, it's certainly unsettling and very intriguing. The implications remain slightly ambiguous, but the article is not needlessly vague or boringly incomprehensible. Solid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it approaches disquieting. Extremely so. There's just enough here to be unsettling, a deep sense of "wrongness". However, I don't feel its strong enough to really carry it. If it was less subtle, there may have felt as to more of a payoff as to what was really going on here. Note (I feel) that the SCP Foundation does poorly when it comes to subtleties. Often, it is more effective to just have the reveal relevealed and give it significant emotional weight so that reader can enjoy the thrill of the reveal regardless.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't amount to much in the end, no real clue as to what's actually going on here, but it's terrifically unsettling, as others have said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the messages placed between the logs, and the fact that you don't really know what is causing the disappearances, just that it appears intelligent and that it is trying to help adds a nice degree of mystery.

If I had one complaint, it would be that you go way overboard with the redaction. If you actually added some names in there instead of blackboxes, I feel the piece would be more organic.

Overall, an unsettling, simple piece. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can understand and appreciate these lines of criticism. I felt the voice itself needed to seem a bit stilted and carry shades of not-actually-caring-beyond-a-superficial-level and for its own purposes, yet still say some of what a person who feels deprived of social interaction could appreciate. Excluding Research Assistant NoNameGiven's precise responses, actions, and terminal commands was deliberate. I didn't want to push the apathy too much more than was done because it had the potential to come off as some sort of compulsion/mind-control effect, which it definitely should not be. Similar point on people having different reactions once they were about to disappear; people have different personalities and universal happiness felt like it would be a compulsion. YMMV on all of the above's execution is perfectly valid though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I do like the core idea and some of the events taking place in the logs, the article drags a lot towards the end. In addition, there's no "end" to the article. Stuff happens, and…that's it. I mean yes, the reader does disappear at the end of the article, but I predicted that the moment the article directly addressed the reader.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one isn't working for me. The various logs are a little too disparate and seemingly random to form a coherent picture I would find it to be an improvement, actually, for there to be a unanimous welcoming of the people-napping. Both the first transcript and the running-in-place seem like they're working against the concept, to me at least. On that note, the running excerpt seems cartoonish. I get what you were going for, and the attempt to translate their experience to an outside perspective is commendable, but it doesn't leave the right impression.

You could stand to push the apathy towards the affected further, and their withdrawal that drives them to this thing. The meta-speak is a little insubstantial, and I do wish there was more to it than a couple smiley-faces. I had high hopes that I was going to walk away unsettled based off the description, but it didn't move me in any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well-balanced - nothing too explicit, but enough odd details and potential double-meaning to have implicit meaning read into it. The uncertainty here is what causes the terror, and I think this does about as good a job of that as might reasonably be expected. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This left me unsettled. I think that some other folks might find the communications interlaced with the logs cheesy but it worked for me. You tried to scare me and succeeded, so +1

item.title: Alone Together
item.content:encoded: Special thanks to Petrograd, psul and Gunpowder0 for looking at drafts of this in the forums, and to MrRonin, Blibby_blobfish, DoctorKane, and hiei1020 for reading drafts of this in chat!
